title: South Florida vs Louisville — First Round Viewing Guide
eventTag: NCAA Tournament • First Round • Buffalo, NY
extraInfo: Winner faces #3 Michigan State or #14 North Dakota State on Saturday

stakes.callout: A team built from scratch after its coach died suddenly, playing with five guys averaging 11+ points, running faster than almost anyone in America. Against a former national champion trying to prove last year's revival wasn't a fluke — but missing its best player to a back injury. Oh, and Louisville's leading scorer used to play at South Florida. This one has layers.

cards.label: For South Florida
cards.value: This is only USF's 4th NCAA Tournament appearance ever. They're playing for their late coach Amir Abdur-Rahim, who passed away in October 2024. The entire roster except one player is new. They're on a 12-game winning streak.

cards.label: For Louisville
cards.value: The Cardinals haven't won an NCAA Tournament game since 2017. Last year they lost in the Round of 64 to Creighton. Another early exit would raise real questions about Pat Kelsey's rebuild.

cards.label: The Injury Factor
cards.value: Five-star freshman Mikel Brown Jr. (18.2 PPG) has a lingering back injury and missed the entire ACC Tournament. He's "day-to-day." If he plays, Louisville is a different team. If not, USF has a real shot.

cards.label: The Conwell Connection
cards.value: Louisville's leading scorer Ryan Conwell started his career at South Florida as a freshman (5.1 PPG). He transferred three times and now averages 18.7 PPG. He'll be facing his old program.

styles.title: Fastest Team in America
bullets: Pace: USF plays at 78.8 possessions per game — the fastest tempo in Division I. They average 87.7 PPG (8th nationally). If you blink, they've already pushed it up the floor and scored.

Balanced scoring: Five players average 11+ PPG. You can't key on one guy because someone else will beat you. Wes Enis (16.8), Izaiyah Nelson (15.7), Joseph Pinion (14.2), Josh Omojafo (11.5), and CJ Brown (11.1) all can go off.
The glass: USF ranks 3rd nationally in rebounding. They overwhelm opponents on the boards and generate extra possessions that compound their pace advantage. This is their superpower.
Late-season defense: Don't let the tempo fool you — since February 1, USF has been a top-25 defense nationally. The roster took time to gel, but once it did, both ends of the floor clicked.
Weakness: Perimeter shooting efficiency is just okay. If Louisville packs the paint and forces USF to win from three, that's where the Bulls are most vulnerable.

styles.title: Three-Point Bombers with Teeth
bullets: Volume shooting: Louisville makes 11.6 threes per game (3rd nationally) with the second-most attempts in the country. Two players have 100+ made threes this season. When they're on, it's a shooting gallery.

The fatal flaw: When the threes aren't falling, Louisville has no Plan B. In their seven losses, they shot just 29% from deep. Conwell and Brown drop to 27% and 25% respectively in losses. Live by the three, die by the three.

Defense is real: This isn't just a shooting team. Louisville ranks 28th in KenPom defensive efficiency. Opponents shoot just 40.3% overall. They're one of only nine teams in America ranked top-25 in both offensive AND defensive efficiency.
Offensive rebounding: Top five nationally. Louisville gets second chances at an elite rate, which is critical when you're shooting 35.7% from three — misses get recycled into more looks.
The Brown variable: With Mikel Brown Jr. healthy, Louisville is a potential Sweet 16 team. Without him, the offense gets significantly less dynamic and the burden on Conwell becomes enormous.

keys.title: The tempo battle will decide this game.
keys.description: USF wants to play at warp speed — 78.8 possessions per game. Louisville is comfortable at a moderate pace. If USF can push the tempo and create chaos, Louisville's half-court shooting advantage shrinks. If Louisville can slow it down and turn this into a half-court game, their superior three-point shooting takes over.

keys.title: Can Louisville handle USF's rebounding?
keys.description: USF ranks 3rd nationally in rebounding. Louisville's top-five offensive rebounding creates an irresistible force vs. immovable object dynamic. Whichever team controls the glass controls the game. Louisville has the edge on the offensive boards; USF has the edge overall. This matchup within the matchup is huge.

keys.title: Is Mikel Brown Jr. playing?
keys.description: Honestly, this might matter more than any tactical matchup. Brown averaged 18.2 PPG and 4.7 APG before his back injury. He missed the entire ACC Tournament. If he plays — even at 70% — Louisville's offensive ceiling goes way up. If he sits, USF's speed and depth could overwhelm a shorthanded Cardinal backcourt.

keys.title: The Conwell revenge game.
keys.description: Ryan Conwell started his career at South Florida, averaging 5.1 PPG as a freshman before transferring three times to become an 18.7 PPG scorer at Louisville. He knows the program. He knows the culture. Revenge games don't always matter — but when they do, they really do. USF can't let him get going early.

keys.title: Five scorers vs. elite defense.
keys.description: Louisville's defense ranks 28th in efficiency, and they're used to facing ACC-caliber scorers. But have they faced five guys who can all go for 15+ on any given night? USF's offensive balance is unusual. Louisville can't just take away one player and feel safe. The Cardinals need to be locked in on all five.

prediction.summary: Everything hinges on Mikel Brown Jr.'s health. With Brown, Louisville has the backcourt firepower (Conwell + Brown = 37 PPG) to outscore anyone. Without him, USF's speed, depth, and 12-game winning streak become overwhelming advantages. Even at less than full strength, Louisville's three-point shooting (11.6 makes per game, 3rd nationally) and 28th-ranked defense make them the slight favorite. But USF's five double-figure scorers and league-best tempo create the kind of chaos that Louisville's three-point-dependent offense struggles with. The Conwell revenge angle adds an emotional wild card.

phases.title: Opening 10 Minutes
phases.description: USF pushes the pace immediately — 78.8 possessions per game, the fastest in Division I. Nelson (15.7 PPG, 9.6 RPG) attacks the glass, and Enis scores in transition off a Louisville turnover. But Louisville answers with threes — McKneely and Hadley (45.6% from deep) both connect. The game is played in the 80s and both teams are comfortable. It's 18-17 Louisville.

phases.title: Mid-First Half
phases.description: Conwell heats up against his former program. There's extra juice in his step, and he hits two contested threes. Louisville's offensive rebounding (top five nationally) generates second chances that compensate for their 35.7% three-point average. But USF's rebounding (3rd nationally) fights back, and Omojafo and Pinion combine for 10 quick points. Halftime is a toss-up: Louisville by 3.

phases.title: Early Second Half
phases.description: USF's late-season defensive improvement (top-25 since February 1) shows up. They tighten the screws on Louisville's perimeter shooters, and the Cardinals go cold from three — in losses, Conwell and Brown Jr. drop to 27% and 25% respectively. CJ Brown — the only player who stayed after the coach's death — steadies the offense with his 4.8 APG and USF takes the lead.

phases.title: Final 5 Minutes
phases.description: Louisville's defense (28th KenPom) locks in. Hadley guards multiple positions, and the Cardinals force a couple of USF turnovers. The three-point barrage returns — McKneely hits a dagger from the wing. If Brown Jr. is playing, even at 70%, he provides the late-game scoring punch that tips it to Louisville. USF's pace keeps them in it until the very end, but Louisville's shooting volume eventually breaks through.

projectedScore.rationale: Louisville's three-point volume and Conwell's revenge-game performance narrowly edge USF's balanced attack. The Brown Jr. injury situation keeps this a coin flip — if he's out, flip these scores.

players.name: Izaiyah Nelson
players.meta: F • AAC Player of the Year, Defensive POY & Tournament MVP
players.bio: Won the historic triple crown of AAC awards — Player of the Year, Defensive Player of the Year, and Tournament MVP in the same season. At 15.7 points and 9.6 rebounds, he does everything. He's the anchor on both ends and the player Louisville has to account for on every possession.

players.name: Wes Enis
players.meta: G • Zero-star recruit • D-II to the Big Dance
players.bio: One of the best stories in the tournament. Enis was a zero-star recruit who played Division II basketball before transferring to USF. Now he's the team's leading scorer at 16.8 PPG and was named the AAC Tournament's Most Outstanding Player. From overlooked to elite — that's the kind of story March is made for.

players.name: CJ Brown
players.meta: G • The only player who stayed
players.bio: When coach Amir Abdur-Rahim passed away in October 2024, most of the roster transferred out. CJ Brown stayed. He's the emotional core of this team, the thread connecting this season to what came before. He leads USF in assists and brings a steadiness that holds everything together.

players.name: Joseph Pinion
players.bio: A former four-star recruit who played at Arkansas, Pinion brings Power Five experience and wing scoring to a roster that needed both. At 14.2 PPG, he's USF's third-leading scorer and provides the kind of perimeter shot-making that can stretch Louisville's defense.

players.name: Ryan Conwell
players.meta: G • Transfer from Xavier • Started career at South Florida
players.bio:
The ultimate transfer portal success story — and this game's most fascinating subplot. Conwell started his career at South Florida (5.1 PPG as a freshman), then went to Indiana State, then Xavier (16.5 PPG), and now leads Louisville at 18.7 PPG. He'll face his old program on Thursday. Three-star recruit turned elite scorer.
Read more →

players.name: Mikel Brown Jr.
players.bio:
The X-factor of the entire game. A five-star McDonald's All-American from Orlando who scored 45 points against NC State on Feb. 9 — breaking the ACC freshman scoring record and tying Louisville's 59-year program record. But a lingering back injury caused him to miss eight games and the entire ACC Tournament. He's "day-to-day." If he plays, everything changes.
Read more →

players.name: Isaac McKneely
players.bio:
From Poca, West Virginia — a two-time Gatorade State Player of the Year who led his high school to a state title. Played three seasons at Virginia, where he led the ACC in 3P% at 42.1%. One of two Cardinals with 100+ made threes this season. Coach Kelsey called him "one of those guys" who terrified opposing coaches.
Read more →

players.name: J'Vonne Hadley
players.meta: G • 6-6 • JUCO to Colorado to Louisville
players.bio: The defensive anchor. Hadley took the long road: Northeastern → Indian Hills CC (NJCAA All-American) → Colorado (Sweet Sixteen) → Louisville. At 6-6, he can guard multiple positions and shoots an absurd .456 from three and .573 overall. He's the kind of experienced, versatile guard who thrives in tournament settings.

funFacts.title: Playing for a Lost Coach (USF)
funFacts.body: USF head coach Amir Abdur-Rahim passed away on October 24, 2024, at age 43 during a medical procedure. Bryan Hodgson rebuilt the roster almost entirely through the portal. The student section was renamed the "Love Wins Classic" in Abdur-Rahim's honor, and the AAC created a sportsmanship award bearing his name.

funFacts.title: Conwell's Full Circle (Louisville)
funFacts.body: Louisville's leading scorer Ryan Conwell started his college career at South Florida (5.1 PPG as a freshman). He transferred three times — USF → Indiana State → Xavier → Louisville — and is now averaging 18.7 PPG. On Thursday, he faces the school that first gave him a chance.

funFacts.title: The Vacated Championship (Louisville)
funFacts.body: Louisville's 2013 national championship was vacated by the NCAA in 2018 — the first vacated basketball title in NCAA history — after a scandal involving former coach Rick Pitino. Pitino now coaches St. John's, which is in Louisville's bracket region. More →

funFacts.title: Foster Care to Coach of the Year (USF)
funFacts.body: USF coach Bryan Hodgson grew up in the foster care system. He worked under Nate Oats at Buffalo and Alabama before becoming a head coach. In his first year at USF, he won AAC Coach of the Year. He's the fourth coach in NCAA history to reach a conference title game in each of his first three head coaching seasons.

funFacts.title: Zero Stars to Leading Scorer (USF)
funFacts.body: Wes Enis was a zero-star recruit who played Division II basketball before landing at USF. Now he leads the team in scoring at 16.8 PPG and was named AAC Tournament Most Outstanding Player. Nobody wanted him. Now he's in the NCAA Tournament.

funFacts.title: From the Bundesliga (Louisville)
funFacts.body: Sananda Fru played four professional seasons in the German Bundesliga before enrolling at Louisville for his first college basketball season. The 6-11 forward from Berlin shoots .759 from the field — one of the highest marks in the country. More →

funFacts.title: 45 Points and a Record (Louisville)
funFacts.body: Freshman Mikel Brown Jr. scored 45 points against NC State on Feb. 9, breaking the ACC freshman scoring record and tying a Louisville program record that stood for 59 years. That same game, Conwell scored 31 — the first 30-point scoring duo in program history.

funFacts.title: Notable Bulls (USF)
funFacts.body: Baseball Hall of Fame manager Tony La Russa earned his law degree at USF. Other notable alumni include journalist and TV host Chris Hansen.

funFacts.title: Founded in 1798 (Louisville)
funFacts.body: The University of Louisville was founded in 1798 — making it one of the oldest public universities in the country and one of the first city-owned universities in America. Among its alumni: Johnny Unitas, Lamar Jackson, and the first female CIA Director (Gina Haspel).

funFacts.title: The Biggest Basketball Arena (Louisville)
funFacts.body: Louisville plays at the KFC Yum! Center — at 22,090 seats, it's the largest arena in the United States designed primarily for basketball. The court is named Denny Crum Court after the Hall of Fame coach who won two national titles for the program (1980, 1986).

funFacts.title: Only Their 4th Time Here (USF)
funFacts.body: This is only USF's 4th NCAA Tournament appearance in program history (1990, 1992, 2012, 2026). Their best run was in 2012 when they won two games. For context, Louisville has 45 tournament appearances.

funFacts.title: The Canadian Connection (USF)
funFacts.body: Josh Omojafo is a Canadian who played D-II basketball before transferring to USF. He averages 11.5 PPG and 5.3 RPG. Like Wes Enis, he made the D-II-to-NCAA-Tournament leap — USF has two guys living that dream.

history.callout: Louisville has 45 NCAA Tournament appearances, three national championships (one vacated), and ten Final Fours. South Florida has been to the tournament three times before this. This is a blue blood trying to reclaim its status against a program making its own history. And the connection between them? Louisville's best player used to wear USF's jersey.

subsections.title: Louisville's Rise, Fall, and Rise Again

timeline.text: Denny Crum wins two national championships, establishing Louisville as a premier program. The 1986 title featured Pervis Ellison, the first freshman named NCAA Tournament Most Outstanding Player.
Under Rick Pitino, Louisville beats Michigan 82-76 to win the national championship. Pitino becomes the first coach to win titles at two different schools.
Pitino fired amid scandal. NCAA vacates the 2013 championship, 123 wins, and 15 tournament victories — the first vacated basketball title in history.
Under Kenny Payne, Louisville goes 12-52 over two seasons, finishing last in the ACC both years. The program hits rock bottom.
Pat Kelsey takes over and engineers a 19-win improvement — the biggest single-season turnaround in program history. Louisville returns to the tournament.
Back in the tournament for the second straight year. A win would be Louisville's first in the NCAA Tournament since 2017.

subsections.title: South Florida's Path to This Moment
subsections.content:
USF's basketball history is short but the current chapter might be its most compelling. The Bulls have only been to the NCAA Tournament three times before: 1990, 1992, and 2012. The 2012 team — coached by Stan Heath — won two games before losing to Ohio, marking the program's deepest run.

This 2026 team was built from the ashes of tragedy. After coach Amir Abdur-Rahim's death in October 2024, Bryan Hodgson inherited a program that had lost almost its entire roster. He rebuilt through the portal, won both the AAC regular season and tournament titles, and led USF to its first tournament appearance in 14 years — all in his first season.

A win over Louisville would match the deepest run in program history and add another remarkable chapter to an already incredible story.

subsections.title: The Conwell Connection
subsections.content:
Ryan Conwell's college journey started at South Florida in 2020-21, where he averaged 5.1 PPG as a freshman. He transferred to Indiana State, then Xavier (where he became a star at 16.5 PPG), and finally Louisville. Now he's the Cardinals' leading scorer at 18.7 PPG.

On Thursday, Conwell will face the program that first recruited him, first believed in him, and first gave him a Division I scholarship. It's a full-circle moment — and the kind of subplot that makes the NCAA Tournament irresistible.
